coredata.dc:title: Effect of zinc oxide films on the structure and photoluminescence properties of graphene-like nanoflakes
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Dang, Chun
Gao, Benling
coredata.dc:description: Graphene-like nanoflakes (GLNFs) were grown on silicon substrates pre-coated with carbon dots and zinc oxide films by hot filament chemical vapor deposition in the methane environment. The structure and composition of GLNFs were studied using field emission scanning electron microscope, micro-Raman spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, respectively. The results indicate that zinc oxide reduces the defects of GLNFs and the reaction of oxygen with carbon. The room temperature photoluminescence (PL) properties of GLNFs were investigated in a Ramalog system using 325nm line of He–Cd laser as an excitation source. The PL results reveal that the GLNFs grown on the silicon substrate pre-coated with carbon dots and ZnO films generate a strong green PL band located at about 514nm. However there is a difference in the PL intensity, which originates from the difference in structure of GLNFs and the distorted and non-bonding states formed in interfaces between GLNFs and ZnO film. The results can contribute to the synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in the area of optical devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene-like nanoflakes
Chemical vapor deposition
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of ZnO on structure and composition of GLNFs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Grant no. 11404278 ). We thank the editorial help of Assoc. Prof. Qijin Cheng who has worked for many years in the University of Sydney.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FESEM image and (b) Raman spectrum of carbon dots.
(a) FESEM image and (b) XRD pattern of ZnO film.
(a)–(c) FESEM images of samples A–C, respectively.
(a) GLNF and (b) ZnO Raman spectra of samples A–C.
C 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of samples A and B.
(1)–(3) PL spectra of samples A–C, respectively.
The positions and intensity ratios of D, G, 2D and D+G peaks.

head.ce:title: Effect of zinc oxide films on the structure and photoluminescence properties of graphene-like nanoflakes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mathematics and Physics, Huaiyin Institute of Technology, 1 Meicheng Road, Huaian, Jiangsu 223002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Mathematics and Physics, Huaiyin Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene-like nanoflakes (GLNFs) were grown on silicon substrates pre-coated with carbon dots and zinc oxide films by hot filament chemical vapor deposition in the methane environment. The structure and composition of GLNFs were studied using field emission scanning electron microscope, micro-Raman spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, respectively. The results indicate that zinc oxide reduces the defects of GLNFs and the reaction of oxygen with carbon. The room temperature photoluminescence (PL) properties of GLNFs were investigated in a Ramalog system using 325nm line of He–Cd laser as an excitation source. The PL results reveal that the GLNFs grown on the silicon substrate pre-coated with carbon dots and ZnO films generate a strong green PL band located at about 514nm. However there is a difference in the PL intensity, which originates from the difference in structure of GLNFs and the distorted and non-bonding states formed in interfaces between GLNFs and ZnO film. The results can contribute to the synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in the area of optical devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene-based nanostructures feature many unique physical and chemical properties such as great surface area, good electrical and thermal conductivity, chemical reactivity, etc., which makes them have a range of applications in nanoelectronics, photonics, sensors, and other areas [1,2]. However, the zero bandgap of perfect graphene restrains some applications of graphene. This is because a bandgap is required when graphene is applied in the areas of microelectronics and optoelectronics [3]. Due to the opening of bandgap of graphene nanoribbons [4], the graphene-like nanoflakes (GLNFs) have attracted much attention in the last years [5–7]. In particular, the photoluminescence (PL) properties of GLNFs have been extensively studied, which indicates that the GLNFs can generate the blue, green and red PL bands [6,7]. In addition, the PL properties of GLNFs can be changed by doping [8]. Furthermore, the hybrid of graphene and zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles can also change the PL properties of graphene [9]. Whether are the structural and PL properties of GLNFs changed if the GLNFs directly grow on ZnO films? This activates us to synthesize the GLNFs on ZnO films and study their PL properties.

In this work, the ZnO films were pre-deposited on silicon substrates by sputtering, and then the GLNFs were directly grown on ZnO films by hot filament chemical vapor deposition (HFCVD) in the methane environment. To comparatively study the structural and PL properties, the GLNFs were also grown on the silicon substrate pre-coated with carbon dots by HFCVD. The PL properties of GLNFs were studied in a Ramalog system using 325nm line of He–Cd laser as an excitation source. The experimental results show that the structural and PL properties of GLNFs are indeed changed by the ZnO films. In this paper, we report the results on the effect of ZnO films on the structural and PL properties of GLNFs.

To obtain the clean surfaces of silicon substrates, the silicon substrates were ultrasonically cleaned in toluene, acetone and ethanol solutions, and then they were boiled in the complex solution of ammonia, aquae hydrogenii dioxide and deionized water as the method described in Ref. [7]. The ZnO films were deposited on the clean silicon substrates by sputtering, and the thickness of deposited ZnO films was about 50nm.

The growth of GLNFs was carried out in a HFCVD system, which is similar to the system described in Ref. [7]. Briefly, the CVD reactor consists of the heating and bias systems. The heating system is made up of tungsten filaments which are used to decompose the reaction gases and heat the substrate. The bias system consists of a DC power supply, which is used to produce a plasma. After the silicon substrate pre-deposited with ZnO film was placed in the CVD reactor, the CVD reactor was evacuated till the pressure was about 2Pa, and then methane gas with a flow rate of 50sccm was introduced into the CVD reactor. Once the pressure was increased to about 2×103 Pa, the evacuation valve was adjusted to stabilize the pressure. Simultaneously, the AC power supply was turned on to heat the filaments. Due to the short distance between the filaments and the substrate (~8mm), the silicon substrate was quickly heated to about 950°C. At this temperature, the GLNFs were grown for several minutes. Two samples were prepared with the growth time of 5 and 7min, respectively.

To comparatively study, the GLNFs were grown on the silicon substrate pre-coated with carbon dots as the method of Ref. [7]. The carbon dots were deposited on silicon surface for 20min. in N2–H2–CH4 plasma, where the flow rates of nitrogen, hydrogen and methane were 30, 50, and 20sccm, and the bias current and growth temperature were 160mA and 850°C, respectively. Then, the GLNFs were grown on the silicon substrate pre-deposited with carbon dots for 5min, which is similar to the above mentioned procedure for the growth of GLNFs on ZnO films.

To conveniently analyze, the sample of GLNFs grown on the silicon substrate pre-coated with carbon dots was named as A and the samples of GLNFs grown on ZnO films for 5 and 7min were named as B and C, respectively.

The structure and composition of GLNFs were investigated by Hatch S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) (operated at 15 KV), HR 800 micro-Raman spectroscope using a 532nm line of semiconductor laser as the excitation source and X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) using an Al Kα X-ray source, respectively. The PL properties of GLNFs were measured in SPEX 1403 Ramalog system using the 325nm line of He–Cd laser as the excitation source.

Fig. 1 shows the FESEM image and Raman spectrum of the synthesized carbon dots. Fig. 1(a) indicates that the carbon dots are non-homogeneously formed on the silicon surface and the size of carbon dots is in a range of 12–60nm. Fig. 1(b) shows that the Raman spectrum is composed of two main peaks located at about 1370 and 1596cm−1, which feature the D and G peaks of carbon nanomaterials, respectively [7].

Fig. 2 shows the FESEM image and XRD pattern of ZnO film. From Fig. 2(a), one can see that the film is composed of nanoparticles with a diameter of about 22–33nm. The strong peak at about 34.32° shown in Fig. 2(b) results from the diffraction of (002) ZnO (JCPDS card 36-1451), which indicates that the ZnO nanoparticles possesses the wurtzite structure [10].

Fig. 3 shows the FESEM images of samples A–C, which indicates that these samples are composed of nanoflakes. From Fig. 3, the edges of GLNFs in samples B and C are longer than those in sample A, indicating that the area of GLNFs in samples B and C is large compared to sample A.

Fig. 4 displays the Raman spectra of samples A–C. In Fig. 4(a), all the spectra show the D, G, 2D and D+G peaks, respectively. From Fig. 4(a), the parameters related to these peaks are obtained and they are summarized in Table 1. Considering the measurement error, the D, G, 2D and D+G peaks can considered to be located at the same positions. For these Raman peaks, the D peak results from the breathing mode of sp 2 carbon, the G peak originates from the first order scattering of E 2g phonons of sp 2 carbon at the Brillouin zone center and the 2D peak (the second order of D peak) is the feature peak of graphene [11,12]. According to Refs. [11,12], the formed nanoflakes feature graphene structure. The data in Table 1 show that the intensity ratio of 2D to G peaks is lower than 2, thus our samples are composed of multilayer graphene sheets. This is because it is generally considered that the intensity ratio of 2D to G peaks is about 2 for the monolayer graphene and that this ratio is reduced with the increase of graphene layers [13]. It is this reason that our samples are termed as graphene-like nanoflakes (GLNFs). From Table 1, one can noticed that the intensity ratios of D to G peaks are 0.91, 0.87 and 0.73, respectively, for samples A–C, which indicates that the defects of samples B and C are fewer than those of sample A because the D peak is associated to defects of graphene [11].

When samples B and C were characterized by Raman spectroscopy, ZnO was checked and their Raman spectra were shown in Fig. 4(b), which similar to the results of Azarang et al. [12]. In Fig. 4(b), every spectrum shows the peaks located at about 92, 118, 263, 435, and 522cm−1, respectively. Among these peaks, the peaks located at about 263 and 522cm−1 are attributed to the silent modes of ZnO [14] and the peak located at about 435cm−1 is related to E 2 mode of high frequency phonons in the ZnO nanostructures [15,16]. For those peaks located at about 92 and 118cm−1, their origin is not clear, which is possible to be the interaction of carbon and zinc atoms. This is because there is a peak located at about 100cm−1 resulting from the E2 mode of low frequency phonons of ZnO films [15,16] and some new Raman peaks appear due to the doping in ZnO films [17].

Fig. 5 shows the C 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of samples A and B. To analyze the bonding states of elements, the spectra are fitted using the standard XPS peak fitting software after Shirley background subtraction. As shown in Fig. 5(a) and (b), the C 1s XPS spectrum of sample A is fitted by two peaks located at about 284.7 and 285.6eV, and the C 1s XPS spectrum of sample B is fitted by two peaks located at about 284.8 and 285.3eV, respectively. Among these peaks, the peaks located at about 284.7 and 284.8eV are attributed to sp 2 C–C bonds [7,18] and the peaks located at about 285.3 and 285.6eV are related to sp 3 C–C bonds [18,19]. From the C 1s XPS spectra, we obtain the ratio of sp 3 to sp 2 carbon and it is about 0.14 and 0.16 for the samples A and B, which indicate that the main component of GLNFs is the sp 2 carbon. According to these data, the ratio of D to G peaks should be low for the samples because the D peak of graphene in the Raman spectrum is related to the defects of graphene [11], however the data in Table 1 show a high ratio of D to G peaks. The high ratio of D to G peaks originate from the edges of GLNFs, this is because the D peak is related to the edges of graphene [11]. Fig. 3 shows that the samples are composed of GLNFs, which implies that the samples contain a number of edges of GLNFs. As a result, the Raman spectra show a high ratio of D to G peaks.

From Fig. 5(c) and (d), the O 1s XPS spectrum of sample A is decomposed into two peaks located at about 532.2 and 533.4eV, while the O 1s XPS spectrum of sample B is decomposed into three peaks located at about 530.9, 531.8 and 532.8eV, respectively. For these peaks, the peak at about 530.9eV is related to the bonding of oxygen with zinc [20,21]. The peak at about 531.8eV is attributed to the hydroxyl or/and carbonyl groups [21,22], however Kotsis and his collaborator attribute it to the bonding of oxygen with zinc [23]. For other peaks at about 532.2, 532.4 and 533.4eV are related to the bonding of oxygen with carbon [7,24], but the peak at about 533.4eV is related to the OH group [23,25].

In addition, the XPS results indicate that the oxygen contents of samples A and B are about 0.92% and 1.1%. The XPS peaks in Fig. 5(c) indicates that oxygen bonds with carbon for the sample A, however Fig. 5(d) shows that some of oxygen is related to ZnO. According to Fig. 5(d), the oxygen content related to ZnO is about 37% if the peak at about 531.8eV is attributed to carbonyl groups [21]. In other words, the oxygen content in the GLNFs of sample B is about 0.7%, which indicates the content of oxygen bonding with carbon in sample B is smaller than that in sample A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ZnO on structure and composition of GLNFs
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis of Section 3.1 indicates that the GLNFs of samples B and C have the large area, a small number of defects, and the reduction of oxygen bonding with carbon compared to the GLNFs of sample A, which implies that ZnO alters the structure and composition of GLNFs.

The area and defects of GLNFs are related to the surface treatment of substrates. From Figs.1 and 2, one can see that there are the carbon dots and ZnO nanoparticles on the silicon substrates of samples A, B and C, respectively. According to the SEM images of Figs.1 and 2, we obtain the densities of carbon dots and ZnO nanoparticles, which are about 142 and 527dot/μm2, respectively. In other words, the silicon surfaces of samples B and C have more heterogeneous islands than sample A. The results of Seo et al. indicate that the heterogeneous islands can act as the nucleation centers of graphene nanosheets [5], thus the silicon surfaces of samples B and C have more nucleation centers of GLNFs compared to the sample A. Due to the same growth conditions, the growth rate of GLNFs of samples B and C is higher than that of sample A so that the area of GLNFs of samples B and C is larger than that of sample A. The increased area of GLNFs results in the reduction of edges of GLNFs, which implies the reduction of defects in the GLNFs. This is confirmed by the ratio reduction of D to G peaks for the Raman spectra of samples B and C in Fig. 4 because the defects in graphene featured by D peak are related to the edges of graphene [11].

As for the reduction of oxygen bonding with carbon in samples B and C, it is relevant to some reactions occurring on the substrate surfaces and the increased area of GLNFs of samples B and C. Due to the high temperature of filaments, methane is decomposed by the hot filaments according to the following reaction [7],
(1) CH4→CH3+H

After the methyl radicals and hydrogen atoms diffuse on the surface of ZnO film under the temperature gradient formed between filaments and substrate, hydrogen react with ZnO,
(2) 2H+ZnO→Zn+H2O

In this case, there are carbon and zinc atoms on the surface of substrate. The residual oxygen in the CVD reactor reacts with the carbon and zinc atoms, and forms zinc oxide and carbon oxide, respectively. For sample A, there is no ZnO film on the surface of silicon substrate. Consequently, the residual oxygen reacts with the carbon atoms and forms carbon oxide. Furthermore, the area of GLNFs in the samples B and C is larger than that in the sample A, i.e. the total edge length of GLNFs in the samples B and C is reduced. Because the edges of graphene are active for absorption [26], the relative reduction of edge length of GLNFs results in the reduction of absorption of oxygen or C–O groups. It is these reasons that the oxygen bonding with carbon for samples B and C is smaller than that for sample A.

Fig. 6 shows the PL spectra of samples A–C. As shown in Fig. 6, the spectrum (1) is composed of two PL bands located at about 514 and 653nm and the spectra (2) and (3) are composed of only one PL band located at about 514nm. For these PL bands, the PL band at about 653nm originates from the second reflection of laser on the grating of the spectrometer because its intensity is very low and the excitation source is the 325nm line of He–Cd laser. The PL band at about 514nm is attributed to the green emission of GLNFs, which is similar to the PL generation of hybrid of ZnO nanoparticles and graphene oxide [12]. According to Fig. 6, we obtain the PL intensity ratio and it is about 1.02:1.14:1 for the spectra (1)–(3), which indicates that the PL intensity of sample B is stronger than the sample A and the PL intensity of sample C is almost the same as the sample A. The generation of green PL band has been studied in Refs. [6 ,7], which originate from the transitions between the π* and π bands. Here, we mainly analyze the difference of PL bands for different samples.

From Table 1, the ratios of 2D to G peaks are about 0.74, 0.86 and 0.81 for the samples A– C, thus the GLNFs in the samples B and C are thinner than that in the sample A because the ratio is reduced with increase of graphene layers [13], i.e., the size of sp 2 carbon particles in the samples B and C is smaller than that in the sample A. As a result, the samples B and C have a wide bandgap compared to the sample A [27]. According to the ratios of 2D to G peaks, the bandgap of sample C is narrower than that of the sample B. Because the PL efficiency is enhanced with the increase of bandgap [28], the expansion bandgap of sample B results in a strong PL intensity compared to the sample A.

According to the Table 1, the ratios of D to G peaks are 0.91, 0.87 and 0.73 for the samples A–C, which imply that the total edges of GLNFs in the sample C is the shortest. That is to say, the area of GLNFs of sample C is larger than that of the samples A and B. As a consequence, the sample C has the large interfaces between GLNFs and ZnO film so that the sample C has more distorted and non-bonding states than the samples A and B. The distorted and non-bonding states serve as the centers of non-radiative recombination [29], thus the PL intensity of sample C is reduced.

In summary, the GLNFs were grown on the silicon substrates pre-deposited with carbon dots and ZnO films in HFCVD system, respectively. The structure, composition and PL properties of GLNFs were studied using FESEM, micro-Raman spectroscopy, XPS, and Ramalog system, respectively. The results indicate that the area, composition, defects and PL properties of GLNFs can be altered by ZnO films, which are related to the improvement of the growth rate of GLNFs, the difference in structure of GLNFs and the distorted and non-bonding states formed in interfaces between GLNFs and ZnO film. These results can contribute to the synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in the area of optical devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Grant no. 11404278). We thank the editorial help of Assoc. Prof. Qijin Cheng who has worked for many years in the University of Sydney.
